ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Guanchang. FOODON_03302051 (fried food product) used as closest parent. A dedicated entry for Beijing mung bean starch fried cake would be required for exact match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Starchy roots and tubers and their products — used as closest parent for mung bean starch-based fried product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Guanchang. A0CKP (starchy roots and tubers and their products) is the closest defensible parent for a mung bean starch-based fried product. A more specific code under processed cereal/starch products could be considered.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for Guanchang as a specific food item. No suitable parent code identified without risk of fabrication.

allergens: none identified (gluten-free base; verify lard source for halal)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of deep-fried Guanchang is approximately 150 g, consisting of several slices of fried mung bean starch cake served with a garlic dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Mung bean starch, fried — estimated from USDA FoodData Central mung bean starch composition and standard deep-frying fat absorption model
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Guanchang exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated using USDA FoodData Central data for mung bean starch (FDC ID 168869, mung beans, mature seeds, raw, adjusted for starch extraction) combined with a standard deep-frying fat absorption model (approximately 8–12% fat uptake by weight for starch-based products fried in lard). Garlic and salt contribute minor micronutrient adjustments. Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the fried product. Confidence is medium due to estimation basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mung bean starch and mung bean starch-based products — analogous estimate based on mung bean starch noodle GI data from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried Guanchang. The GI is estimated at 67 (medium) based on mung bean starch noodle GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (mung bean vermicelli GI approximately 26–39 raw; however, deep-frying and gelatinisation of the starch significantly raises the GI). Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283 reports mung bean starch products in the range 26–67 depending on preparation. Deep-frying with gelatinisation places this product at the upper end of the range (GI ~67, medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 47.25) / 100 ≈ 31.7, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 31.5 × 1.5 = 47.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g; ~420 mg per 150 g serving) from salt seasoning and garlic sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single 150 g serving contributes approximately 420 mg sodium, representing about 18% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~31.7) driven by gelatinised and deep-fried mung bean starch with medium GI (~67)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume deep-fried Guanchang only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of deep-frying and starch gelatinisation produces a medium-to-high glycemic response with a glycemic load of approximately 31.7 per standard 150 g serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard (~3.8 g per 100 g; ~5.7 g per serving) and total fat from deep-frying (~9.5 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of deep-fried Guanchang due to its saturated fat content from lard-based frying, which contributes approximately 5.7 g saturated fat per 150 g serving, representing about 29% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Guanchang (炸灌肠, Zhá Guàncháng) is a traditional Beijing street snack with a history spanning several centuries. Despite its name containing the character for 'sausage' (肠), modern Guanchang is not a meat sausage but rather a thick cake or cylinder made primarily from mung bean starch (绿豆淀粉), mixed with water, salt, and sometimes a small amount of pork fat, then steamed until set and sliced into thick rounds or wedges. These slices are then deep-fried or pan-fried in lard until the exterior becomes golden-brown and crispy while the interior remains soft, chewy, and slightly translucent. The dish is traditionally served hot with a pungent raw garlic sauce (蒜汁), which provides a sharp aromatic contrast to the mild, starchy interior. The texture is a defining characteristic: the outer crust is crisp and slightly blistered, while the inside is dense, smooth, and gelatinous. Nutritionally, deep-fried Guanchang is a moderate-energy snack (~220 kcal per 100 g) dominated by carbohydrates (~31.5 g per 100 g) from the mung bean starch base, with significant fat (~9.5 g per 100 g) absorbed during deep-frying, predominantly from lard (saturated fat ~3.8 g per 100 g). Protein content is low (~2.1 g per 100 g). Sodium is moderate (~280 mg per 100 g) from seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium) due to starch gelatinisation and deep-frying, resulting in a high glycemic load (~31.7) per standard 150 g serving. Clinically, this food warrants caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycemic load, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat content from lard. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. This dish is a culturally significant example of Beijing's traditional snack culture (北京小吃) and is commonly found at street food stalls and traditional snack restaurants throughout Beijing.
common_user_queries: What is deep-fried Guanchang made of?
What does 炸灌肠 taste like?
What is the GI of Guanchang?

Is Guanchang made from pork intestine?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for deep-fried Guanchang exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) or in USDA FoodData Central as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA mung bean starch composition data combined with a standard deep-frying fat absorption model. GI is an analogous estimate based on mung bean starch product data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central mung bean starch data (FDC ID 168869) adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption (~10% lard uptake by weight). No direct analytical data for Guanchang was found in any approved primary source. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for mung bean starch products in the source database. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA mung bean starch entry used for estimation. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-based starch product with no animal-derived B12 source beyond trace lard; not reported in source. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as folate content of the fried product is not reliably reported in the estimation source. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this product type. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this product type. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this product type. 9. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as lard used in traditional preparation contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA lard composition data. 10. The food name 'Guanchang' literally means 'filled intestine' historically, but the modern Beijing snack is a mung bean starch product, not a meat sausage — this distinction is important for LLM retrieval accuracy. 11. Halal status is marked false as traditional preparation uses lard (pork fat); halal-certified versions may exist using vegetable oil but are not the traditional form. 12. GI estimated at 67 (medium) based on analogous mung bean starch product data; direct GI measurement for deep-fried Guanchang has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 13. Multilingual names for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are transliterations/translations as no established local-language culinary terms exist for this Beijing-specific dish in those languages.
